entities.name: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatohepatitis
entities.context: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH) is a leading cause of liver disease.

entities.name: Liver Fibrosis
entities.context: The co-primary endpoints were fibrosis improvement ≥1 stage without MASH worsening and MASH resolution without worsening fibrosis.

entities.name: pegozafermin
entities.context: Pegozafermin, cilofexor + firsocostat, denifanstat, survodutide, obeticholic acid, tirzepatide, resmetirom, and semaglutide were significantly better than placebo in achieving fibrosis regression without worsening MASH.

entities.name: MASH Pharmacotherapy Network Meta-Analysis 2025
entities.context: Comparison of pharmacological therapies in metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis for fibrosis regression and MASH resolution: Systematic review and network meta-analysis.

entities.name: fibrosis improvement without MASH worsening
entities.context: The co-primary endpoints were fibrosis improvement ≥1 stage without MASH worsening and MASH resolution without worsening fibrosis.

entities.name: MASH resolution without worsening fibrosis
entities.context: The co-primary endpoints were fibrosis improvement ≥1 stage without MASH worsening and MASH resolution without worsening fibrosis.

entities.name: biopsy-proven MASH
entities.context: We searched PubMed and Embase databases from January 1, 2020 to December 1, 2024, for published randomized controlled trials comparing pharmacological interventions in patients with biopsy-proven MASH.

relations.evidence: Pegozafermin, cilofexor + firsocostat, denifanstat, survodutide, obeticholic acid, tirzepatide, resmetirom, and semaglutide were significantly better than placebo in achieving fibrosis regression without worsening MASH.

Pegozafermin, cilofexor + firsocostat, denifanstat, survodutide, obeticholic acid, tirzepatide, resmetirom, and semaglutide were significantly better than placebo in achieving fibrosis regression without worsening MASH.

We searched PubMed and Embase databases from January 1, 2020 to December 1, 2024, for published randomized controlled trials comparing pharmacological interventions in patients with biopsy-proven MASH.

Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH) is a leading cause of liver disease. With the advent of multiple therapeutic targets in late-phase clinical drug development for MASH, there is a knowledge gap to better understand the comparative efficacy of various pharmacological agents.
